Asama no Okami | hiro = | script_name = Japanese | script = 木花咲耶姫
木花開耶姫 | affiliation = {{ill|Blossom|en|Blossom|WD=Q2047589|lt=Blossom}}s, {{ill|Life|en|Life|WD=Q3|lt=Life}}, {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji}}, {{ill|Volcano|en|Volcano|WD=Q8072|lt=Volcano}}es | cult_center = [[Sengen shrine|Asama Shrine]]s | abodes = {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji}} | planet = | mantra = | mantra benefits = | weapon = | battles = | artifacts = | animals = | symbol = {{ill|Cherry blossom|en|Cherry blossom|WD=Q871991|lt=''Sakura'' cherry blossom}} | adherents = | height = | age = | tree = | day = | color = | number = | consort =[[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]]; {{ill|Ninigi|WD=Q1056017}}; {{ill|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944}}| parents =[[Ōyamatsumi]]; {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|WD=Q386563}}| siblings = [[Iwanaga-hime]] | offspring ={{ill|Hoderi|en|Hoderi|WD=Q2716491|lt=Hoderi}}, [[Hosuseri]], [[Hoori]]; {{ill|Hoderi|WD=Q2716491}}; {{ill|Honoakari|WD=Q60673696}}; {{ill|Honosusori|WD=Q10731395}}; {{ill|Hoori|WD=Q1051960}}| predecessor = | successor = | army = | mount = | texts = ''[[Kojiki]]'', ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' | gender =Female; {{ill|female|WD=Q6581072}}| Greek_equivalent = | Roman_equivalent = | Etruscan_equivalent = | Christian_equivalent = | Slavic_equivalent = | Hinduism_equivalent = | Canaanite_equivalent = | equivalent1_type = | equivalent1 = | equivalent2_type = | equivalent2 = | region = {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} | ethnic_group = | festivals = | nirvana = }} '''Konohanasakuya-hime''' is the {{ill|Goddess|en|Goddess|WD=Q205985|lt=Goddess}} of {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji}} and all volcanoes in [[Japanese mythology]]; she is also the blossom-princess and symbol of delicate earthly life.''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book I, page 71. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}{{Cite book|last1=Coulter|first1=Charles Russell|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=sEIngqiKOugC&q=fuji|title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities|last2=Turner|first2=Patricia|date=2013-07-04|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-96390-3|language=en}} She is often considered an avatar of Japanese life, especially since her symbol is the ''[[:en:Sakura|Sakura]]'' (cherry blossom). [[Shinto]] shrines have been built on Mount Fuji for Sakuya-hime, collectively known as the [[Sengen shrine|Asama or Sengen Shrines]]. It is believed that she will keep Mount Fuji from erupting,"In A.D. 806 a local official built a shrine near the foot of the volcano to keep it from erupting. The priests assigned the task of pacifying the mountain apparently neglected their duties because Fuji erupted with great violence in 864, causing much damage in a nearby province. The governor of that province blamed the priests for failing to perform the proper rites and constructed another shrine in his own territory, where he could make sure everything was done correctly. A fiery god of the mountain became at a later date the more peaceful Shinto goddess of Mount Fuji-- Konohana Sakuya Hime-- the Goddess of Flowering Trees." "Konohana Sakuya Hime originally had little or no connection with Mount Fuji. Sometime between the 14th and 16th centuries, the belief arose among the people of the region that she would protect them from eruptions of the volcano as she had her newborn son from the flames of the burning bower." Konohana is now the principal goddess of Mount Fuji. Members of Fuji-ko have altars in their own home in which they worship Konohana Sakuya Hime. This group also lights a torch for Konohana Sakuya Hime at the fire ceremony at Fuji-Yoshida. but shrines to her at {{ill|Mount Kirishima|en|Mount Kirishima|WD=Q1319361|lt=Kirishima}} have been repeatedly destroyed by [[:en:Volcanic|Volcanic]] eruptions. She is also known for having torn up the [[Yatsugatake Mountains]], because it was higher than Fujiyama. == Name and etymology == Konohanasakuya-hime or Konohananosakuya-hime ( or ; lit. "''[cherry] tree blossom blooming princess''" (''princess'', or ''lady of high birth'' explains {{ill|Hime|en|Hime|WD=Q3889712|lt=Hime}}; her name also appears in a shorter form as "''Sakuya-hime''",{{cite web|title=Creation stories|url=http://www.gly.uga.edu/railsback/CS/CSJapan.html|access-date=20 January 2016|website=gly.uga.edu/|archive-date=27 January 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160127071536/http://www.gly.uga.edu/railsback/CS/CSJapan.html|url-status=dead}} and she is also called ''Sengen''{{cite web|author=Cartwright, M ark|date=12 April 2017|title=Mount Fuji|url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Mount_Fuji/|access-date=16 August 2019|publisher=Ancient History}})) == Myths == === Ninigi and Sakuya-hime === Sakuya-hime met [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] on the seashore and they fell in love; Ninigi asked [[Ōyamatsumi|Ohoyamatsumi]], the father of Sakuya-hime for her hand in marriage. Oho-Yama proposed his older daughter, Iwa-Naga-hime, instead, but Ninigi had his heart set on Sakuya-hime. Oho-Yama reluctantly agreed and Ninigi and Ko-no-hana married. Because Ninigi refused Iwa-Naga, the rock-princess, human lives are said to be short and fleeting, like the ''sakura'' blossoms, instead of enduring and long lasting, like stones. Sakuya-hime became pregnant in just one night, causing suspicion in Ninigi. He wondered if this was the child of another [[kami]]. Sakuya-hime was enraged at Ninigi's accusation and entered a doorless hut, which she then set fire to, declaring that the child would not be hurt if it were truly the offspring of Ninigi. Inside the hut, Ko-no-hana had three sons, {{ill|Hoderi|en|Hoderi|WD=Q2716491|lt=Hoderi}}, [[Hosuseri]] and [[Hoori]].''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book II, page 73. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}"According to the 'Kojiki', the great 8th century A.D. compilation of Japanese mythology, Konohana Sakuya-hime married a god who grew suspicious of her when she became pregnant shortly after their wedding. To prove her fidelity to her husband, she entered a benign {{ill|Bedroom|en|Bedroom|WD=Q193837|lt=bower}} and miraculously gave birth to a son, unscathed by the surrounding flames. The fire ceremony at Fuji-Yyoshida recalls this story as a means of protecting the town from fire and promoting easy childbirth among women." === Yosoji's camellia tree === {{One source section | date = August 2021 }}[[File:YOSOJI'S CAMELLIA TREE.jpg|left|thumb|Konohanasakuya-hime guiding Yosoji to the stream
{{right|{{small|—illustration by {{ill|Ella Du Cane|en|Ella Du Cane|WD=Q5364488|lt=Ella Du Cane}}}}}}]] According to an ddth-century legend, a small village in the {{ill|Suruga Province|en|Suruga Province|WD=Q864170|lt=Suruga Province}} named Kamiide was struck by {{ill|Smallpox|en|Smallpox|WD=Q12214|lt=Smallpox}} {{ill|Epidemic|en|Epidemic|WD=Q44512|lt=Epidemic}}. In the village, there lived a young boy named Yosoji and his mother, who was infected by the disease. To cure his mother, Yosoji went to a fortune teller who told him to give his mother water from a small {{ill|Stream|en|Stream|WD=Q47521|lt=Stream}} near {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji}}. [[File:Fuji goddess flies.jpg|thumb|right|Konohanasakuya-hime flying]] The next day, Yosoji decided to go to the river, but then stumbled upon three different paths, not knowing which one to take. A young girl in white came towards Yosoji from the forest and guided him to the stream. Yosoji scooped up water from the stream and gave it to his mother to drink. Days later, Yosoji went back to the area where the three paths met, and met the girl in white once again. She told him to come back in three days, and that she would meet him there. She also told him that it would take five trips to the stream to treat all the villagers. Five trips later, Yosoji's mother was healed, and so were many other villagers. The villagers thanked him, but he knew that he owed it all to the girl in white. So he went back to the stream to give thanks to the girl in white. However, he found that the stream was completely dried up and the girl was nowhere to be seen. Thus, Yosoji prayed that the girl would reveal herself, so that he might thank her for her actions. The girl appeared before him and Yosoji said that he wished to tell her how deeply grateful he was to her and that he wished to know who she was, so he could tell the villagers whom to thank. The girl in white said that her identity was not important and said farewell to Yosoji. She then swung a branch of {{ill|Camellia|en|Camellia|WD=Q212815|lt=Camellia}} over her head and a cloud from Mount Fuji came down and picked her up. Yosoji realized the woman was none other than the Goddess of Mount Fuji.{{cite book|title=Ancient Tales and Folklore of Japan|author=[[Richard Gordon Smith]]|year=1908|chapter-url=https://books.google.com/books?id=HlAqAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA189|chapter=Yosoji's Camellia Tree|others=Illustrated by {{ill|Ella Du Cane|en|Ella Du Cane|WD=Q5364488|lt=Ella Du Cane}}|via=Google Books}} ==Genealogy== {{Three generations of Hyuga}} ==In popular culture== In ''[[Persona 4]]'' Konohana Sakuya is a persona owned by {{ill|Yukiko Amagi|en|Yukiko Amagi|WD=Q89513574|lt=Yukiko Amagi}}.{{cite book |title=Persona 4 Visual Data |publisher=Atlus |year=2008 |page=60}} == References == {{reflist}} == External links == {{wiktionary|Konohananosakuya-hime}} * {{cite web|title=FUJISANHONGU SENGENTAISHA| language=English| url=http://www.fuji-hongu.or.jp/sengen/english/index.html| accessdate = 2021-03-01}} - Official homepage of the Asama shrines near Mount Fuji {{jmyth navbox long}} {{Asama Faith}} {{Iwanaga Faith}} [[wiktionary:木花咲耶姫|木花咲耶姫]] [[wiktionary:木花開耶姫|木花開耶姫]] [[en:Konohanasakuya-hime]] [[az:Konohanasakuya]] [[ca:Konohana no Sakuya hime]] {{Commons category|Konohanasakuya-hime}} [[de:Konohanasakuyahime]] [[fa:کونوهاناساکویا-هیمه]] [[fr:Konohana-no-Sakuya]] [[ja:コノハナノサクヤビメ]] [[ko:고노하나노사쿠야비메]] [[mni:ꯀꯣꯅꯣꯍꯅꯁꯀꯨꯌ-ꯍꯤꯃꯦ]] [[pl:Konohanasakuya-hime]] [[ru:Коноханасакуя-химэ]] [[sv:Konohanasakuyahime]] [[th:เซ็นเง็ง]] [[vi:Konohana Sakuya Hime]] [[zh:木花開耶姬]] {{wikidata link|Q1781862}} [[Category:Asama shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Fuji worship]] [[Category:Japanese goddesses]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Mount Fuji]] [[Category:Mountain goddesses]] [[Category:Nature goddesses]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Volcano goddesses]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

木花開耶姫 | affiliation = {{ill|Blossom|en|Blossom|WD=Q2047589|lt=Blossom}}s, {{ill|Life|en|Life|WD=Q3|lt=Life}}, {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji}}, {{ill|Volcano|en|Volcano|WD=Q8072|lt=Volcano}}es | cult_center = [[Sengen shrine|Asama Shrine]]s | abodes = {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji}} | planet = | mantra = | mantra benefits = | weapon = | battles = | artifacts = | animals = | symbol = {{ill|Cherry blossom|en|Cherry blossom|WD=Q871991|lt=''Sakura'' cherry blossom}} | adherents = | height = | age = | tree = | day = | color = | number = | consort =[[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]]; {{ill|Ninigi|WD=Q1056017}}; {{ill|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944}}| parents =[[Ōyamatsumi]]; {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|WD=Q386563}}| siblings = [[Iwanaga-hime]] | offspring ={{ill|Hoderi|en|Hoderi|WD=Q2716491|lt=Hoderi}}, [[Hosuseri]], [[Hoori]]; {{ill|Hoderi|WD=Q2716491}}; {{ill|Honoakari|WD=Q60673696}}; {{ill|Honosusori|WD=Q10731395}}; {{ill|Hoori|WD=Q1051960}}| predecessor = | successor = | army = | mount = | texts = ''[[Kojiki]]'', ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' | gender =Female; {{ill|female|WD=Q6581072}}| Greek_equivalent = | Roman_equivalent = | Etruscan_equivalent = | Christian_equivalent = | Slavic_equivalent = | Hinduism_equivalent = | Canaanite_equivalent = | equivalent1_type = | equivalent1 = | equivalent2_type = | equivalent2 = | region = {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} | ethnic_group = | festivals = | nirvana = }} '''Konohanasakuya-hime''' is the {{ill|Goddess|en|Goddess|WD=Q205985|lt=Goddess}} of {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji}} and all volcanoes in [[Japanese mythology]]; she is also the blossom-princess and symbol of delicate earthly life.''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book I, page 71. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}{{Cite book|last1=Coulter|first1=Charles Russell|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=sEIngqiKOugC&q=fuji|title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities|last2=Turner|first2=Patricia|date=2013-07-04|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-96390-3|language=en}} She is often considered an avatar of Japanese life, especially since her symbol is the ''[[:en:Sakura|Sakura]]'' (cherry blossom). [[Shinto]] shrines have been built on Mount Fuji for Sakuya-hime, collectively known as the [[Sengen shrine|Asama or Sengen Shrines]]. It is believed that she will keep Mount Fuji from erupting,"In A.D. 806 a local official built a shrine near the foot of the volcano to keep it from erupting. The priests assigned the task of pacifying the mountain apparently neglected their duties because Fuji erupted with great violence in 864, causing much damage in a nearby province. The governor of that province blamed the priests for failing to perform the proper rites and constructed another shrine in his own territory, where he could make sure everything was done correctly. A fiery god of the mountain became at a later date the more peaceful Shinto goddess of Mount Fuji-- Konohana Sakuya Hime-- the Goddess of Flowering Trees." "Konohana Sakuya Hime originally had little or no connection with Mount Fuji. Sometime between the 14th and 16th centuries, the belief arose among the people of the region that she would protect them from eruptions of the volcano as she had her newborn son from the flames of the burning bower." Konohana is now the principal goddess of Mount Fuji. Members of Fuji-ko have altars in their own home in which they worship Konohana Sakuya Hime. This group also lights a torch for Konohana Sakuya Hime at the fire ceremony at Fuji-Yoshida. but shrines to her at {{ill|Mount Kirishima|en|Mount Kirishima|WD=Q1319361|lt=Kirishima}} have been repeatedly destroyed by [[:en:Volcanic|Volcanic]] eruptions. She is also known for having torn up the [[Yatsugatake Mountains]], because it was higher than Fujiyama. == Name and etymology == Konohanasakuya-hime or Konohananosakuya-hime ( or ; lit. "''[cherry] tree blossom blooming princess''" (''princess'', or ''lady of high birth'' explains {{ill|Hime|en|Hime|WD=Q3889712|lt=Hime}}; her name also appears in a shorter form as "''Sakuya-hime''",{{cite web|title=Creation stories|url=http://www.gly.uga.edu/railsback/CS/CSJapan.html|access-date=20 January 2016|website=gly.uga.edu/|archive-date=27 January 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160127071536/http://www.gly.uga.edu/railsback/CS/CSJapan.html|url-status=dead}} and she is also called ''Sengen''{{cite web|author=Cartwright, M ark|date=12 April 2017|title=Mount Fuji|url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Mount_Fuji/|access-date=16 August 2019|publisher=Ancient History}})) == Myths == === Ninigi and Sakuya-hime === Sakuya-hime met [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] on the seashore and they fell in love; Ninigi asked [[Ōyamatsumi|Ohoyamatsumi]], the father of Sakuya-hime for her hand in marriage. Oho-Yama proposed his older daughter, Iwa-Naga-hime, instead, but Ninigi had his heart set on Sakuya-hime. Oho-Yama reluctantly agreed and Ninigi and Ko-no-hana married. Because Ninigi refused Iwa-Naga, the rock-princess, human lives are said to be short and fleeting, like the ''sakura'' blossoms, instead of enduring and long lasting, like stones. Sakuya-hime became pregnant in just one night, causing suspicion in Ninigi. He wondered if this was the child of another [[kami]]. Sakuya-hime was enraged at Ninigi's accusation and entered a doorless hut, which she then set fire to, declaring that the child would not be hurt if it were truly the offspring of Ninigi. Inside the hut, Ko-no-hana had three sons, {{ill|Hoderi|en|Hoderi|WD=Q2716491|lt=Hoderi}}, [[Hosuseri]] and [[Hoori]].''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book II, page 73. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}"According to the 'Kojiki', the great 8th century A.D. compilation of Japanese mythology, Konohana Sakuya-hime married a god who grew suspicious of her when she became pregnant shortly after their wedding. To prove her fidelity to her husband, she entered a benign {{ill|Bedroom|en|Bedroom|WD=Q193837|lt=bower}} and miraculously gave birth to a son, unscathed by the surrounding flames. The fire ceremony at Fuji-Yyoshida recalls this story as a means of protecting the town from fire and promoting easy childbirth among women." === Yosoji's camellia tree === {{One source section | date = August 2021 }}[[File:YOSOJI'S CAMELLIA TREE.jpg|left|thumb|Konohanasakuya-hime guiding Yosoji to the stream
{{right|{{small|—illustration by {{ill|Ella Du Cane|en|Ella Du Cane|WD=Q5364488|lt=Ella Du Cane}}}}}}]] According to an ddth-century legend, a small village in the {{ill|Suruga Province|en|Suruga Province|WD=Q864170|lt=Suruga Province}} named Kamiide was struck by {{ill|Smallpox|en|Smallpox|WD=Q12214|lt=Smallpox}} {{ill|Epidemic|en|Epidemic|WD=Q44512|lt=Epidemic}}. In the village, there lived a young boy named Yosoji and his mother, who was infected by the disease. To cure his mother, Yosoji went to a fortune teller who told him to give his mother water from a small {{ill|Stream|en|Stream|WD=Q47521|lt=Stream}} near {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji}}. [[File:Fuji goddess flies.jpg|thumb|right|Konohanasakuya-hime flying]] The next day, Yosoji decided to go to the river, but then stumbled upon three different paths, not knowing which one to take. A young girl in white came towards Yosoji from the forest and guided him to the stream. Yosoji scooped up water from the stream and gave it to his mother to drink. Days later, Yosoji went back to the area where the three paths met, and met the girl in white once again. She told him to come back in three days, and that she would meet him there. She also told him that it would take five trips to the stream to treat all the villagers. Five trips later, Yosoji's mother was healed, and so were many other villagers. The villagers thanked him, but he knew that he owed it all to the girl in white. So he went back to the stream to give thanks to the girl in white. However, he found that the stream was completely dried up and the girl was nowhere to be seen. Thus, Yosoji prayed that the girl would reveal herself, so that he might thank her for her actions. The girl appeared before him and Yosoji said that he wished to tell her how deeply grateful he was to her and that he wished to know who she was, so he could tell the villagers whom to thank. The girl in white said that her identity was not important and said farewell to Yosoji. She then swung a branch of {{ill|Camellia|en|Camellia|WD=Q212815|lt=Camellia}} over her head and a cloud from Mount Fuji came down and picked her up. Yosoji realized the woman was none other than the Goddess of Mount Fuji.{{cite book|title=Ancient Tales and Folklore of Japan|author=[[Richard Gordon Smith]]|year=1908|chapter-url=https://books.google.com/books?id=HlAqAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA189|chapter=Yosoji's Camellia Tree|others=Illustrated by {{ill|Ella Du Cane|en|Ella Du Cane|WD=Q5364488|lt=Ella Du Cane}}|via=Google Books}} ==Genealogy== {{Three generations of Hyuga}} ==In popular culture== In ''[[Persona 4]]'' Konohana Sakuya is a persona owned by {{ill|Yukiko Amagi|en|Yukiko Amagi|WD=Q89513574|lt=Yukiko Amagi}}.{{cite book |title=Persona 4 Visual Data |publisher=Atlus |year=2008 |page=60}} == References == {{reflist}} == External links == {{wiktionary|Konohananosakuya-hime}} * {{cite web|title=FUJISANHONGU SENGENTAISHA| language=English| url=http://www.fuji-hongu.or.jp/sengen/english/index.html| accessdate = 2021-03-01}} - Official homepage of the Asama shrines near Mount Fuji {{jmyth navbox long}} {{Asama Faith}} {{Iwanaga Faith}} [[wiktionary:木花咲耶姫|木花咲耶姫]] [[wiktionary:木花開耶姫|木花開耶姫]] [[en:Konohanasakuya-hime]] [[az:Konohanasakuya]] [[ca:Konohana no Sakuya hime]] {{Commons category|Konohanasakuya-hime}} [[de:Konohanasakuyahime]] [[fa:کونوهاناساکویا-هیمه]] [[fr:Konohana-no-Sakuya]] [[ja:コノハナノサクヤビメ]] [[ko:고노하나노사쿠야비메]] [[mni:ꯀꯣꯅꯣꯍꯅꯁꯀꯨꯌ-ꯍꯤꯃꯦ]] [[pl:Konohanasakuya-hime]] [[ru:Коноханасакуя-химэ]] [[sv:Konohanasakuyahime]] [[th:เซ็นเง็ง]] [[vi:Konohana Sakuya Hime]] [[zh:木花開耶姬]] {{wikidata link|Q1781862}} [[Category:Asama shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Fuji worship]] [[Category:Japanese goddesses]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Mount Fuji]] [[Category:Mountain goddesses]] [[Category:Nature goddesses]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Volcano goddesses]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages]]

Oho-Yama proposed his older daughter, Iwa-Naga-hime, instead, but Ninigi had his heart set on Sakuya-hime. Oho-Yama reluctantly agreed and Ninigi and Ko-no-hana married. Sakuya-hime became pregnant in just one night, causing suspicion in Ninigi. He wondered if this was the child of another [[kami]]. Sakuya-hime was enraged at Ninigi's accusation and entered a doorless hut, which she then set fire to, declaring that the child would not be hurt if it were truly the offspring of Ninigi. Inside the hut, Ko-no-hana had three sons, {{ill|Hoderi|en|Hoderi|WD=Q2716491|lt=Hoderi|1=Hoderi}}, [[Hosuseri]] and [[Hoori]].''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book II, page 73. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}"According to the 'Kojiki', the great 8th century A.D. compilation of Japanese mythology, Konohana Sakuya-hime married a god who grew suspicious of her when she became pregnant shortly after their wedding. The fire ceremony at Fuji-Yyoshida recalls this story as a means of protecting the town from fire and promoting easy childbirth among women." === Yosoji's camellia tree === {{One source section | date = August 2021 }}[[File:YOSOJI'S CAMELLIA TREE.jpg|left|thumb|Konohanasakuya-hime guiding Yosoji to the stream
{{right|{{small|—illustration by {{ill|Ella Du Cane|en|Ella Du Cane|WD=Q5364488|lt=Ella Du Cane|1=Ella Du Cane}}}}}}]] According to an ddth-century legend, a small village in the {{ill|Suruga Province|en|Suruga Province|WD=Q864170|lt=Suruga Province|1=Suruga Province}} named Kamiide was struck by {{ill|Smallpox|en|Smallpox|WD=Q12214|lt=Smallpox|1=Smallpox}} {{ill|Epidemic|en|Epidemic|WD=Q44512|lt=Epidemic|1=Epidemic}}. In the village, there lived a young boy named Yosoji and his mother, who was infected by the disease. To cure his mother, Yosoji went to a fortune teller who told him to give his mother water from a small {{ill|Stream|en|Stream|WD=Q47521|lt=Stream|1=Stream}} near {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji|1=Mount Fuji}}. [[File:Fuji goddess flies.jpg|thumb|right|Konohanasakuya-hime flying]] The next day, Yosoji decided to go to the river, but then stumbled upon three different paths, not knowing which one to take. A young girl in white came towards Yosoji from the forest and guided him to the stream. Yosoji scooped up water from the stream and gave it to his mother to drink. She also told him that it would take five trips to the stream to treat all the villagers. Five trips later, Yosoji's mother was healed, and so were many other villagers. The villagers thanked him, but he knew that he owed it all to the girl in white. So he went back to the stream to give thanks to the girl in white. However, he found that the stream was completely dried up and the girl was nowhere to be seen. Thus, Yosoji prayed that the girl would reveal herself, so that he might thank her for her actions. The girl appeared before him and Yosoji said that he wished to tell her how deeply grateful he was to her and that he wished to know who she was, so he could tell the villagers whom to thank. The girl in white said that her identity was not important and said farewell to Yosoji. She then swung a branch of {{ill|Camellia|en|Camellia|WD=Q212815|lt=Camellia|1=Camellia}} over her head and a cloud from Mount Fuji came down and picked her up. Yosoji realized the woman was none other than the Goddess of Mount Fuji.{{cite book|title=Ancient Tales and Folklore of Japan|author=[[Richard Gordon Smith]]|year=1908|chapter-url=https://books.google.com/books?id=HlAqAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA189|chapter=Yosoji's Camellia Tree|others=Illustrated by {{ill|Ella Du Cane|en|Ella Du Cane|WD=Q5364488|lt=Ella Du Cane|1=Ella Du Cane}}|via=Google Books}} ==Genealogy== {{Three generations of Hyuga}} ==In popular culture== In ''[[Persona 4]]'' Konohana Sakuya is a persona owned by {{ill|Yukiko Amagi|en|Yukiko Amagi|WD=Q89513574|lt=Yukiko Amagi|1=Yukiko Amagi}}.{{cite book |title=Persona 4 Visual Data |publisher=Atlus |year=2008 |page=60}} == References == {{reflist}} == External links == {{wiktionary|Konohananosakuya-hime}} * {{cite web|title=FUJISANHONGU SENGENTAISHA| language=English| url=http://www.fuji-hongu.or.jp/sengen/english/index.html| accessdate = 2021-03-01}} - Official homepage of the Asama shrines near Mount Fuji {{jmyth navbox long}} {{Asama Faith}} {{Iwanaga Faith}} [[wiktionary:木花咲耶姫|木花咲耶姫]] [[wiktionary:木花開耶姫|木花開耶姫]] [[en:Konohanasakuya-hime]] [[az:Konohanasakuya]] [[ca:Konohana no Sakuya hime]] {{Commons category|Konohanasakuya-hime}} [[de:Konohanasakuyahime]] [[fa:کونوهاناساکویا-هیمه]] [[fr:Konohana-no-Sakuya]] [[ja:コノハナノサクヤビメ]] [[ko:고노하나노사쿠야비메]] [[mni:ꯀꯣꯅꯣꯍꯅꯁꯀꯨꯌ-ꯍꯤꯃꯦ]] [[pl:Konohanasakuya-hime]] [[ru:Коноханасакуя-химэ]] [[sv:Konohanasakuyahime]] [[th:เซ็นเง็ง]] [[vi:Konohana Sakuya Hime]] [[zh:木花開耶姬]] {{wikidata link|Q1781862}} [[Category:Asama shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Fuji worship]] [[Category:Japanese goddesses]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Mount Fuji]] [[Category:Mountain goddesses]] [[Category:Nature goddesses]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Volcano goddesses]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages]]

Asama no Okami | hiro = | script_name = Japanese | script = 木花咲耶姫
木花開耶姫 | affiliation = {{ill|Blossom|en|Blossom|WD=Q2047589|lt=Blossom|1=Blossom}}s, {{ill|Life|en|Life|WD=Q3|lt=Life|1=Life}}, {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji|1=Mount Fuji}}, {{ill|Volcano|en|Volcano|WD=Q8072|lt=Volcano|1=Volcano}}es | cult_center = [[Sengen shrine|Asama Shrine]]s | abodes = {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji|1=Mount Fuji}} | planet = | mantra = | mantra benefits = | weapon = | battles = | artifacts = | animals = | symbol = {{ill|Cherry blossom|en|Cherry blossom|WD=Q871991|lt=''Sakura'' cherry blossom|1=Cherry blossom}} | adherents = | height = | age = | tree = | day = | color = | number = | consort =[[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]]; {{ill|Ninigi|WD=Q1056017|1=Ninigi-no-Mikoto}}; {{ill|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}}| parents =[[Ōyamatsumi]]; {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|WD=Q386563|1=Ōyamatsumi}}| siblings = [[Iwanaga-hime]] | offspring ={{ill|Hoderi|en|Hoderi|WD=Q2716491|lt=Hoderi|1=Hoderi}}, [[Hosuseri]], [[Hoori]]; {{ill|Hoderi|WD=Q2716491|1=Hoderi}}; {{ill|Honoakari|WD=Q60673696|1=Honoakari}}; {{ill|Honosusori|WD=Q10731395|1=Hosuseri}}; {{ill|Hoori|WD=Q1051960|1=Hoori}}| predecessor = | successor = | army = | mount = | texts = ''[[Kojiki]]'', ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' | gender =Female; {{ill|female|WD=Q6581072|1=Female (gender)}}| Greek_equivalent = | Roman_equivalent = | Etruscan_equivalent = | Christian_equivalent = | Slavic_equivalent = | Hinduism_equivalent = | Canaanite_equivalent = | equivalent1_type = | equivalent1 = | equivalent2_type = | equivalent2 = | region = {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | ethnic_group = | festivals = | nirvana = }} '''Konohanasakuya-hime''' is the {{ill|Goddess|en|Goddess|WD=Q205985|lt=Goddess|1=Goddess}} of {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji|1=Mount Fuji}} and all volcanoes in [[Japanese mythology]]; she is also the blossom-princess and symbol of delicate earthly life.''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book I, page 71. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}{{Cite book|last1=Coulter|first1=Charles Russell|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=sEIngqiKOugC&q=fuji|title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities|last2=Turner|first2=Patricia|date=2013-07-04|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-96390-3|language=en}} She is often considered an avatar of Japanese life, especially since her symbol is the ''[[:en:Sakura|Sakura]]'' (cherry blossom). [[Shinto]] shrines have been built on Mount Fuji for Sakuya-hime, collectively known as the [[Sengen shrine|Asama or Sengen Shrines]]. It is believed that she will keep Mount Fuji from erupting,"In A.D. 806 a local official built a shrine near the foot of the volcano to keep it from erupting. The priests assigned the task of pacifying the mountain apparently neglected their duties because Fuji erupted with great violence in 864, causing much damage in a nearby province. The governor of that province blamed the priests for failing to perform the proper rites and constructed another shrine in his own territory, where he could make sure everything was done correctly. A fiery god of the mountain became at a later date the more peaceful Shinto goddess of Mount Fuji-- Konohana Sakuya Hime-- the Goddess of Flowering Trees." "Konohana Sakuya Hime originally had little or no connection with Mount Fuji. Sometime between the 14th and 16th centuries, the belief arose among the people of the region that she would protect them from eruptions of the volcano as she had her newborn son from the flames of the burning bower." Konohana is now the principal goddess of Mount Fuji. Members of Fuji-ko have altars in their own home in which they worship Konohana Sakuya Hime. This group also lights a torch for Konohana Sakuya Hime at the fire ceremony at Fuji-Yoshida. but shrines to her at {{ill|Mount Kirishima|en|Mount Kirishima|WD=Q1319361|lt=Kirishima|1=Mount Kirishima}} have been repeatedly destroyed by [[:en:Volcanic|Volcanic]] eruptions. She is also known for having torn up the [[Yatsugatake Mountains]], because it was higher than Fujiyama. == Name and etymology == Konohanasakuya-hime or Konohananosakuya-hime ( or ; lit. "''[cherry] tree blossom blooming princess''" (''princess'', or ''lady of high birth'' explains {{ill|Hime|en|Hime|WD=Q3889712|lt=Hime|1=Hime}}; her name also appears in a shorter form as "''Sakuya-hime''",{{cite web|title=Creation stories|url=http://www.gly.uga.edu/railsback/CS/CSJapan.html|access-date=20 January 2016|website=gly.uga.edu/|archive-date=27 January 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160127071536/http://www.gly.uga.edu/railsback/CS/CSJapan.html|url-status=dead}} and she is also called ''Sengen''{{cite web|author=Cartwright, M ark|date=12 April 2017|title=Mount Fuji|url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Mount_Fuji/|access-date=16 August 2019|publisher=Ancient History}})) == Myths == === Ninigi and Sakuya-hime === Sakuya-hime met [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] on the seashore and they fell in love; Ninigi asked [[Ōyamatsumi|Ohoyamatsumi]], the father of Sakuya-hime for her hand in marriage. Oho-Yama proposed his older daughter, Iwa-Naga-hime, instead, but Ninigi had his heart set on Sakuya-hime. Oho-Yama reluctantly agreed and Ninigi and Ko-no-hana married. Because Ninigi refused Iwa-Naga, the rock-princess, human lives are said to be short and fleeting, like the ''sakura'' blossoms, instead of enduring and long lasting, like stones. Sakuya-hime became pregnant in just one night, causing suspicion in Ninigi. He wondered if this was the child of another [[kami]]. Sakuya-hime was enraged at Ninigi's accusation and entered a doorless hut, which she then set fire to, declaring that the child would not be hurt if it were truly the offspring of Ninigi. Inside the hut, Ko-no-hana had three sons, {{ill|Hoderi|en|Hoderi|WD=Q2716491|lt=Hoderi|1=Hoderi}}, [[Hosuseri]] and [[Hoori]].''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book II, page 73. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}"According to the 'Kojiki', the great 8th century A.D. compilation of Japanese mythology, Konohana Sakuya-hime married a god who grew suspicious of her when she became pregnant shortly after their wedding. To prove her fidelity to her husband, she entered a benign {{ill|Bedroom|en|Bedroom|WD=Q193837|lt=bower|1=Bedroom}} and miraculously gave birth to a son, unscathed by the surrounding flames. The fire ceremony at Fuji-Yyoshida recalls this story as a means of protecting the town from fire and promoting easy childbirth among women." === Yosoji's camellia tree === {{One source section | date = August 2021 }}[[File:YOSOJI'S CAMELLIA TREE.jpg|left|thumb|Konohanasakuya-hime guiding Yosoji to the stream
{{right|{{small|—illustration by {{ill|Ella Du Cane|en|Ella Du Cane|WD=Q5364488|lt=Ella Du Cane|1=Ella Du Cane}}}}}}]] According to an ddth-century legend, a small village in the {{ill|Suruga Province|en|Suruga Province|WD=Q864170|lt=Suruga Province|1=Suruga Province}} named Kamiide was struck by {{ill|Smallpox|en|Smallpox|WD=Q12214|lt=Smallpox|1=Smallpox}} {{ill|Epidemic|en|Epidemic|WD=Q44512|lt=Epidemic|1=Epidemic}}. In the village, there lived a young boy named Yosoji and his mother, who was infected by the disease. To cure his mother, Yosoji went to a fortune teller who told him to give his mother water from a small {{ill|Stream|en|Stream|WD=Q47521|lt=Stream|1=Stream}} near {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji|1=Mount Fuji}}. [[File:Fuji goddess flies.jpg|thumb|right|Konohanasakuya-hime flying]] The next day, Yosoji decided to go to the river, but then stumbled upon three different paths, not knowing which one to take. A young girl in white came towards Yosoji from the forest and guided him to the stream. Yosoji scooped up water from the stream and gave it to his mother to drink. Days later, Yosoji went back to the area where the three paths met, and met the girl in white once again. She told him to come back in three days, and that she would meet him there. She also told him that it would take five trips to the stream to treat all the villagers. Five trips later, Yosoji's mother was healed, and so were many other villagers. The villagers thanked him, but he knew that he owed it all to the girl in white. So he went back to the stream to give thanks to the girl in white. However, he found that the stream was completely dried up and the girl was nowhere to be seen. Thus, Yosoji prayed that the girl would reveal herself, so that he might thank her for her actions. The girl appeared before him and Yosoji said that he wished to tell her how deeply grateful he was to her and that he wished to know who she was, so he could tell the villagers whom to thank. The girl in white said that her identity was not important and said farewell to Yosoji. She then swung a branch of {{ill|Camellia|en|Camellia|WD=Q212815|lt=Camellia|1=Camellia}} over her head and a cloud from Mount Fuji came down and picked her up. Yosoji realized the woman was none other than the Goddess of Mount Fuji.{{cite book|title=Ancient Tales and Folklore of Japan|author=[[Richard Gordon Smith]]|year=1908|chapter-url=https://books.google.com/books?id=HlAqAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA189|chapter=Yosoji's Camellia Tree|others=Illustrated by {{ill|Ella Du Cane|en|Ella Du Cane|WD=Q5364488|lt=Ella Du Cane|1=Ella Du Cane}}|via=Google Books}} ==Genealogy== {{Three generations of Hyuga}} ==In popular culture== In ''[[Persona 4]]'' Konohana Sakuya is a persona owned by {{ill|Yukiko Amagi|en|Yukiko Amagi|WD=Q89513574|lt=Yukiko Amagi|1=Yukiko Amagi}}.{{cite book |title=Persona 4 Visual Data |publisher=Atlus |year=2008 |page=60}} == References == {{reflist}} == External links == {{wiktionary|Konohananosakuya-hime}} * {{cite web|title=FUJISANHONGU SENGENTAISHA| language=English| url=http://www.fuji-hongu.or.jp/sengen/english/index.html| accessdate = 2021-03-01}} - Official homepage of the Asama shrines near Mount Fuji {{jmyth navbox long}} {{Asama Faith}} {{Iwanaga Faith}} [[wiktionary:木花咲耶姫|木花咲耶姫]] [[wiktionary:木花開耶姫|木花開耶姫]] [[en:Konohanasakuya-hime]] [[az:Konohanasakuya]] [[ca:Konohana no Sakuya hime]] {{Commons category|Konohanasakuya-hime}} [[de:Konohanasakuyahime]] [[fa:کونوهاناساکویا-هیمه]] [[fr:Konohana-no-Sakuya]] [[ja:コノハナノサクヤビメ]] [[ko:고노하나노사쿠야비메]] [[mni:ꯀꯣꯅꯣꯍꯅꯁꯀꯨꯌ-ꯍꯤꯃꯦ]] [[pl:Konohanasakuya-hime]] [[ru:Коноханасакуя-химэ]] [[sv:Konohanasakuyahime]] [[th:เซ็นเง็ง]] [[vi:Konohana Sakuya Hime]] [[zh:木花開耶姬]] {{wikidata link|Q1781862}} [[Category:Asama shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Fuji worship]] [[Category:Japanese goddesses]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Mount Fuji]] [[Category:Mountain goddesses]] [[Category:Nature goddesses]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Volcano goddesses]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages]]

This group also lights a torch for Konohana Sakuya Hime at the fire ceremony at Fuji-Yoshida. but shrines to her at {{ill|Mount Kirishima|en|Mount Kirishima|WD=Q1319361|lt=Kirishima|1=Mount Kirishima}} have been repeatedly destroyed by [[:en:Volcanic|Volcanic]] eruptions. She is also known for having torn up the [[Yatsugatake Mountains]], because it was higher than Fujiyama. == Name and etymology == Konohanasakuya-hime or Konohananosakuya-hime ( or ; lit. "''[cherry] tree blossom blooming princess''" (''princess'', or ''lady of high birth'' explains {{ill|Hime|en|Hime|WD=Q3889712|lt=Hime|1=Hime}}; her name also appears in a shorter form as "''Sakuya-hime''",{{cite web|title=Creation stories|url=http://www.gly.uga.edu/railsback/CS/CSJapan.html|access-date=20 January 2016|website=gly.uga.edu/|archive-date=27 January 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160127071536/http://www.gly.uga.edu/railsback/CS/CSJapan.html|url-status=dead}} and she is also called ''Sengen''{{cite web|author=Cartwright, M ark|date=12 April 2017|title=Mount Fuji|url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Mount_Fuji/|access-date=16 August 2019|publisher=Ancient History}})) == Myths == === Ninigi and Sakuya-hime === Sakuya-hime met [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] on the seashore and they fell in love; Ninigi asked [[Ōyamatsumi|Ohoyamatsumi]], the father of Sakuya-hime for her hand in marriage. Oho-Yama proposed his older daughter, Iwa-Naga-hime, instead, but Ninigi had his heart set on Sakuya-hime. Oho-Yama reluctantly agreed and Ninigi and Ko-no-hana married. Because Ninigi refused Iwa-Naga, the rock-princess, human lives are said to be short and fleeting, like the ''sakura'' blossoms, instead of enduring and long lasting, like stones. Sakuya-hime became pregnant in just one night, causing suspicion in Ninigi. He wondered if this was the child of another [[kami]]. Sakuya-hime was enraged at Ninigi's accusation and entered a doorless hut, which she then set fire to, declaring that the child would not be hurt if it were truly the offspring of Ninigi. Inside the hut, Ko-no-hana had three sons, {{ill|Hoderi|en|Hoderi|WD=Q2716491|lt=Hoderi|1=Hoderi}}, [[Hosuseri]] and [[Hoori]].''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book II, page 73. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}"According to the 'Kojiki', the great 8th century A.D. compilation of Japanese mythology, Konohana Sakuya-hime married a god who grew suspicious of her when she became pregnant shortly after their wedding. To prove her fidelity to her husband, she entered a benign {{ill|Bedroom|en|Bedroom|WD=Q193837|lt=bower|1=Bedroom}} and miraculously gave birth to a son, unscathed by the surrounding flames. The fire ceremony at Fuji-Yyoshida recalls this story as a means of protecting the town from fire and promoting easy childbirth among women." === Yosoji's camellia tree === {{One source section | date = August 2021 }}[[File:YOSOJI'S CAMELLIA TREE.jpg|left|thumb|Konohanasakuya-hime guiding Yosoji to the stream
{{right|{{small|—illustration by {{ill|Ella Du Cane|en|Ella Du Cane|WD=Q5364488|lt=Ella Du Cane|1=Ella Du Cane}}}}}}]] According to an ddth-century legend, a small village in the {{ill|Suruga Province|en|Suruga Province|WD=Q864170|lt=Suruga Province|1=Suruga Province}} named Kamiide was struck by {{ill|Smallpox|en|Smallpox|WD=Q12214|lt=Smallpox|1=Smallpox}} {{ill|Epidemic|en|Epidemic|WD=Q44512|lt=Epidemic|1=Epidemic}}. In the village, there lived a young boy named Yosoji and his mother, who was infected by the disease. To cure his mother, Yosoji went to a fortune teller who told him to give his mother water from a small {{ill|Stream|en|Stream|WD=Q47521|lt=Stream|1=Stream}} near {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji|1=Mount Fuji}}. [[File:Fuji goddess flies.jpg|thumb|right|Konohanasakuya-hime flying]] The next day, Yosoji decided to go to the river, but then stumbled upon three different paths, not knowing which one to take. A young girl in white came towards Yosoji from the forest and guided him to the stream. Yosoji scooped up water from the stream and gave it to his mother to drink. Days later, Yosoji went back to the area where the three paths met, and met the girl in white once again. She told him to come back in three days, and that she would meet him there. She also told him that it would take five trips to the stream to treat all the villagers. Five trips later, Yosoji's mother was healed, and so were many other villagers. The villagers thanked him, but he knew that he owed it all to the girl in white. So he went back to the stream to give thanks to the girl in white. However, he found that the stream was completely dried up and the girl was nowhere to be seen. Thus, Yosoji prayed that the girl would reveal herself, so that he might thank her for her actions. The girl appeared before him and Yosoji said that he wished to tell her how deeply grateful he was to her and that he wished to know who she was, so he could tell the villagers whom to thank. The girl in white said that her identity was not important and said farewell to Yosoji. She then swung a branch of {{ill|Camellia|en|Camellia|WD=Q212815|lt=Camellia|1=Camellia}} over her head and a cloud from Mount Fuji came down and picked her up. Yosoji realized the woman was none other than the Goddess of Mount Fuji.{{cite book|title=Ancient Tales and Folklore of Japan|author=[[Richard Gordon Smith]]|year=1908|chapter-url=https://books.google.com/books?id=HlAqAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA189|chapter=Yosoji's Camellia Tree|others=Illustrated by {{ill|Ella Du Cane|en|Ella Du Cane|WD=Q5364488|lt=Ella Du Cane|1=Ella Du Cane}}|via=Google Books}} ==Genealogy== {{Three generations of Hyuga}} ==In popular culture== In ''[[Persona 4]]'' Konohana Sakuya is a persona owned by {{ill|Yukiko Amagi|en|Yukiko Amagi|WD=Q89513574|lt=Yukiko Amagi|1=Yukiko Amagi}}.{{cite book |title=Persona 4 Visual Data |publisher=Atlus |year=2008 |page=60}} == References == {{reflist}} == External links == {{wiktionary|Konohananosakuya-hime}} * {{cite web|title=FUJISANHONGU SENGENTAISHA| language=English| url=http://www.fuji-hongu.or.jp/sengen/english/index.html| accessdate = 2021-03-01}} - Official homepage of the Asama shrines near Mount Fuji {{jmyth navbox long}} {{Asama Faith}} {{Iwanaga Faith}} [[wiktionary:木花咲耶姫|木花咲耶姫]] [[wiktionary:木花開耶姫|木花開耶姫]] [[en:Konohanasakuya-hime]] [[az:Konohanasakuya]] [[ca:Konohana no Sakuya hime]] {{Commons category|Konohanasakuya-hime}} [[de:Konohanasakuyahime]] [[fa:کونوهاناساکویا-هیمه]] [[fr:Konohana-no-Sakuya]] [[ja:コノハナノサクヤビメ]] [[ko:고노하나노사쿠야비메]] [[mni:ꯀꯣꯅꯣꯍꯅꯁꯀꯨꯌ-ꯍꯤꯃꯦ]] [[pl:Konohanasakuya-hime]] [[ru:Коноханасакуя-химэ]] [[sv:Konohanasakuyahime]] [[th:เซ็นเง็ง]] [[vi:Konohana Sakuya Hime]] [[zh:木花開耶姬]] {{wikidata link|Q1781862}} [[Category:Asama shrines]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:Fuji worship]] [[Category:Japanese goddesses]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Mount Fuji]] [[Category:Mountain goddesses]] [[Category:Nature goddesses]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Volcano goddesses]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages]]

she is also the blossom-princess and symbol of delicate earthly life.''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book I, page 71. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}{{Cite book|last1=Coulter|first1=Charles Russell|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=sEIngqiKOugC&q=fuji|title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities|last2=Turner|first2=Patricia|date=2013-07-04|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-96390-3|language=en}} She is often considered an avatar of Japanese life, especially since her symbol is the ''[[:en:Sakura|Sakura]]'' (cherry blossom). [[Shinto]] shrines have been built on Mount Fuji for Sakuya-hime, collectively known as the [[Sengen shrine|Asama or Sengen Shrines]]. It is believed that she will keep Mount Fuji from erupting,"In A.D. 806 a local official built a shrine near the foot of the volcano to keep it from erupting. The priests assigned the task of pacifying the mountain apparently neglected their duties because Fuji erupted with great violence in 864, causing much damage in a nearby province. The governor of that province blamed the priests for failing to perform the proper rites and constructed another shrine in his own territory, where he could make sure everything was done correctly. A fiery god of the mountain became at a later date the more peaceful Shinto goddess of Mount Fuji-- Konohana Sakuya Hime-- the Goddess of Flowering Trees." "Konohana Sakuya Hime originally had little or no connection with Mount Fuji. Sometime between the 14th and 16th centuries, the belief arose among the people of the region that she would protect them from eruptions of the volcano as she had her newborn son from the flames of the burning bower." Konohana is now the principal goddess of Mount Fuji. Members of Fuji-ko have altars in their own home in which they worship Konohana Sakuya Hime. This group also lights a torch for Konohana Sakuya Hime at the fire ceremony at Fuji-Yoshida. but shrines to her at {{ill|Mount Kirishima|en|Mount Kirishima|WD=Q1319361|lt=Kirishima|1=Mount Kirishima}} have been repeatedly destroyed by [[:en:Volcanic|Volcanic]] eruptions. She is also known for having torn up the [[Yatsugatake Mountains]], because it was higher than Fujiyama. == Name and etymology == Konohanasakuya-hime or Konohananosakuya-hime ( or ; lit. "''[cherry] tree blossom blooming princess''" (''princess'', or ''lady of high birth'' explains {{ill|Hime|en|Hime|WD=Q3889712|lt=Hime|1=Hime}}; her name also appears in a shorter form as "''Sakuya-hime''",{{cite web|title=Creation stories|url=http://www.gly.uga.edu/railsback/CS/CSJapan.html|access-date=20 January 2016|website=gly.uga.edu/|archive-date=27 January 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160127071536/http://www.gly.uga.edu/railsback/CS/CSJapan.html|url-status=dead}} and she is also called ''Sengen''{{cite web|author=Cartwright, M ark|date=12 April 2017|title=Mount Fuji|url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Mount_Fuji/|access-date=16 August 2019|publisher=Ancient History}})) == Myths == === Ninigi and Sakuya-hime === Sakuya-hime met [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] on the seashore and they fell in love; Ninigi asked [[Ōyamatsumi|Ohoyamatsumi]], the father of Sakuya-hime for her hand in marriage. Oho-Yama proposed his older daughter, Iwa-Naga-hime, instead, but Ninigi had his heart set on Sakuya-hime. Oho-Yama reluctantly agreed and Ninigi and Ko-no-hana married. Because Ninigi refused Iwa-Naga, the rock-princess, human lives are said to be short and fleeting, like the ''sakura'' blossoms, instead of enduring and long lasting, like stones. Sakuya-hime became pregnant in just one night, causing suspicion in Ninigi. He wondered if this was the child of another [[kami]]. Sakuya-hime was enraged at Ninigi's accusation and entered a doorless hut, which she then set fire to, declaring that the child would not be hurt if it were truly the offspring of Ninigi. Inside the hut, Ko-no-hana had three sons, {{ill|Hoderi|en|Hoderi|WD=Q2716491|lt=Hoderi|1=Hoderi}}, [[Hosuseri]] and [[Hoori]].''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. She then swung a branch of {{ill|Camellia|en|Camellia|WD=Q212815|lt=Camellia|1=Camellia}} over her head and a cloud from Mount Fuji came down and picked her up. Yosoji realized the woman was none other than the Goddess of Mount Fuji.{{cite book|title=Ancient Tales and Folklore of Japan|author=[[Richard Gordon Smith]]|year=1908|chapter-url=https://books.google.com/books?id=HlAqAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA189|chapter=Yosoji's Camellia Tree|others=Illustrated by {{ill|Ella Du Cane|en|Ella Du Cane|WD=Q5364488|lt=Ella Du Cane|1=Ella Du Cane}}|via=Google Books}} ==Genealogy== {{Three generations of Hyuga}} ==In popular culture== In ''[[Persona 4]]'' Konohana Sakuya is a persona owned by {{ill|Yukiko Amagi|en|Yukiko Amagi|WD=Q89513574|lt=Yukiko Amagi|1=Yukiko Amagi}}.{{cite book |title=Persona 4 Visual Data |publisher=Atlus |year=2008 |page=60}} == References == {{reflist}} == External links == {{wiktionary|Konohananosakuya-hime}} * {{cite web|title=FUJISANHONGU SENGENTAISHA| language=English| url=http://www.fuji-hongu.or.jp/sengen/english/index.html| accessdate = 2021-03-01}} - Official homepage of the Asama shrines near Mount Fuji {{jmyth navbox long}} {{Asama Faith}} {{Iwanaga Faith}} [[wiktionary:木花咲耶姫|木花咲耶姫]] [[wiktionary:木花開耶姫|木花開耶姫]] [[en:Konohanasakuya-hime]] [[az:Konohanasakuya]] [[ca:Konohana no Sakuya hime]] {{Commons category|Konohanasakuya-hime}} [[de:Konohanasakuyahime]] [[fa:کونوهاناساکویا-هیمه]] [[fr:Konohana-no-Sakuya]] [[ja:コノハナノサクヤビメ]] [[ko:고노하나노사쿠야비메]] [[mni:ꯀꯣꯅꯣꯍꯅꯁꯀꯨꯌ-ꯍꯤꯃꯦ]] [[pl:Konohanasakuya-hime]] [[ru:Коноханасакуя-химэ]] [[sv:Konohanasakuyahime]] [[th:เซ็นเง็ง]] [[vi:Konohana Sakuya Hime]] [[zh:木花開耶姬]] {{wikidata link|Q1781862}} [[Category:Asama shrines]] [[Category:Fuji worship]] [[Category:Japanese goddesses]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Mount Fuji]] [[Category:Mountain goddesses]] [[Category:Nature goddesses]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Volcano goddesses]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages]]

Sakuya-hime
Sengen
Asama no Okami | hiro = | script_name = Japanese | script = 木花咲耶姫
木花開耶姫 | affiliation = {{ill|Blossom|en|Blossom|WD=Q2047589|lt=Blossom|1=Blossom}}s, {{ill|Life|en|Life|WD=Q3|lt=Life|1=Life}}, {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji|1=Mount Fuji}}, {{ill|Volcano|en|Volcano|WD=Q8072|lt=Volcano|1=Volcano}}es | cult_center = [[Sengen shrine|Asama Shrine]]s | abodes = {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji|1=Mount Fuji}} | planet = | mantra = | mantra benefits = | weapon = | battles = | artifacts = | animals = | symbol = {{ill|Cherry blossom|en|Cherry blossom|WD=Q871991|lt=''Sakura'' cherry blossom|1=Cherry blossom}} | adherents = | height = | age = | tree = | day = | color = | number = | consort =[[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]]; {{ill|Ninigi|WD=Q1056017|1=Ninigi-no-Mikoto}}; {{ill|Ōkuninushi|WD=Q276944|1=Ōkuninushi}}| parents =[[Ōyamatsumi]]; {{ill|Ōyamatsumi|WD=Q386563|1=Ōyamatsumi}}| siblings = [[Iwanaga-hime]] | offspring ={{ill|Hoderi|en|Hoderi|WD=Q2716491|lt=Hoderi|1=Hoderi}}, [[Hosuseri]], [[Hoori]]; {{ill|Hoderi|WD=Q2716491|1=Hoderi}}; {{ill|Honoakari|WD=Q60673696|1=Honoakari}}; {{ill|Honosusori|WD=Q10731395|1=Hosuseri}}; {{ill|Hoori|WD=Q1051960|1=Hoori}}| predecessor = | successor = | army = | mount = | texts = ''[[Kojiki]]'', ''[[Nihon Shoki]]'' | gender =Female; {{ill|female|WD=Q6581072|1=Female (gender)}}| Greek_equivalent = | Roman_equivalent = | Etruscan_equivalent = | Christian_equivalent = | Slavic_equivalent = | Hinduism_equivalent = | Canaanite_equivalent = | equivalent1_type = | equivalent1 = | equivalent2_type = | equivalent2 = | region = {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | ethnic_group = | festivals = | nirvana = }} '''Konohanasakuya-hime''' is the {{ill|Goddess|en|Goddess|WD=Q205985|lt=Goddess|1=Goddess}} of {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji|1=Mount Fuji}} and all volcanoes in [[Japanese mythology]]; she is also the blossom-princess and symbol of delicate earthly life.''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book I, page 71. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}{{Cite book|last1=Coulter|first1=Charles Russell|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=sEIngqiKOugC&q=fuji|title=Encyclopedia of Ancient Deities|last2=Turner|first2=Patricia|date=2013-07-04|publisher=Routledge|isbn=978-1-135-96390-3|language=en}} She is often considered an avatar of Japanese life, especially since her symbol is the ''[[:en:Sakura|Sakura]]'' (cherry blossom). [[Shinto]] shrines have been built on Mount Fuji for Sakuya-hime, collectively known as the [[Sengen shrine|Asama or Sengen Shrines]]. It is believed that she will keep Mount Fuji from erupting,"In A.D. 806 a local official built a shrine near the foot of the volcano to keep it from erupting. The priests assigned the task of pacifying the mountain apparently neglected their duties because Fuji erupted with great violence in 864, causing much damage in a nearby province. The governor of that province blamed the priests for failing to perform the proper rites and constructed another shrine in his own territory, where he could make sure everything was done correctly. A fiery god of the mountain became at a later date the more peaceful Shinto goddess of Mount Fuji-- Konohana Sakuya Hime-- the Goddess of Flowering Trees." "Konohana Sakuya Hime originally had little or no connection with Mount Fuji. Sometime between the 14th and 16th centuries, the belief arose among the people of the region that she would protect them from eruptions of the volcano as she had her newborn son from the flames of the burning bower." Konohana is now the principal goddess of Mount Fuji. Members of Fuji-ko have altars in their own home in which they worship Konohana Sakuya Hime. This group also lights a torch for Konohana Sakuya Hime at the fire ceremony at Fuji-Yoshida. but shrines to her at {{ill|Mount Kirishima|en|Mount Kirishima|WD=Q1319361|lt=Kirishima|1=Mount Kirishima}} have been repeatedly destroyed by [[:en:Volcanic|Volcanic]] eruptions. She is also known for having torn up the [[Yatsugatake Mountains]], because it was higher than Fujiyama. == Name and etymology == Konohanasakuya-hime or Konohananosakuya-hime ( or ; lit. "''[cherry] tree blossom blooming princess''" (''princess'', or ''lady of high birth'' explains {{ill|Hime|en|Hime|WD=Q3889712|lt=Hime|1=Hime}}; her name also appears in a shorter form as "''Sakuya-hime''",{{cite web|title=Creation stories|url=http://www.gly.uga.edu/railsback/CS/CSJapan.html|access-date=20 January 2016|website=gly.uga.edu/|archive-date=27 January 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160127071536/http://www.gly.uga.edu/railsback/CS/CSJapan.html|url-status=dead}} and she is also called ''Sengen''{{cite web|author=Cartwright, M ark|date=12 April 2017|title=Mount Fuji|url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Mount_Fuji/|access-date=16 August 2019|publisher=Ancient History}})) == Myths == === Ninigi and Sakuya-hime === Sakuya-hime met [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] on the seashore and they fell in love; Ninigi asked [[Ōyamatsumi|Ohoyamatsumi]], the father of Sakuya-hime for her hand in marriage. Oho-Yama proposed his older daughter, Iwa-Naga-hime, instead, but Ninigi had his heart set on Sakuya-hime. Oho-Yama reluctantly agreed and Ninigi and Ko-no-hana married. Because Ninigi refused Iwa-Naga, the rock-princess, human lives are said to be short and fleeting, like the ''sakura'' blossoms, instead of enduring and long lasting, like stones. Sakuya-hime became pregnant in just one night, causing suspicion in Ninigi. He wondered if this was the child of another [[kami]]. Sakuya-hime was enraged at Ninigi's accusation and entered a doorless hut, which she then set fire to, declaring that the child would not be hurt if it were truly the offspring of Ninigi. Inside the hut, Ko-no-hana had three sons, {{ill|Hoderi|en|Hoderi|WD=Q2716491|lt=Hoderi|1=Hoderi}}, [[Hosuseri]] and [[Hoori]].''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book II, page 73. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}"According to the 'Kojiki', the great 8th century A.D. compilation of Japanese mythology, Konohana Sakuya-hime married a god who grew suspicious of her when she became pregnant shortly after their wedding. To prove her fidelity to her husband, she entered a benign {{ill|Bedroom|en|Bedroom|WD=Q193837|lt=bower|1=Bedroom}} and miraculously gave birth to a son, unscathed by the surrounding flames. The fire ceremony at Fuji-Yyoshida recalls this story as a means of protecting the town from fire and promoting easy childbirth among women." === Yosoji's camellia tree === {{One source section | date = August 2021 }}[[File:YOSOJI'S CAMELLIA TREE.jpg|left|thumb|Konohanasakuya-hime guiding Yosoji to the stream
{{right|{{small|—illustration by {{ill|Ella Du Cane|en|Ella Du Cane|WD=Q5364488|lt=Ella Du Cane|1=Ella Du Cane}}}}}}]] According to an ddth-century legend, a small village in the {{ill|Suruga Province|en|Suruga Province|WD=Q864170|lt=Suruga Province|1=Suruga Province}} named Kamiide was struck by {{ill|Smallpox|en|Smallpox|WD=Q12214|lt=Smallpox|1=Smallpox}} {{ill|Epidemic|en|Epidemic|WD=Q44512|lt=Epidemic|1=Epidemic}}. In the village, there lived a young boy named Yosoji and his mother, who was infected by the disease. To cure his mother, Yosoji went to a fortune teller who told him to give his mother water from a small {{ill|Stream|en|Stream|WD=Q47521|lt=Stream|1=Stream}} near {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji|1=Mount Fuji}}. [[File:Fuji goddess flies.jpg|thumb|right|Konohanasakuya-hime flying]] The next day, Yosoji decided to go to the river, but then stumbled upon three different paths, not knowing which one to take. A young girl in white came towards Yosoji from the forest and guided him to the stream. Yosoji scooped up water from the stream and gave it to his mother to drink. Days later, Yosoji went back to the area where the three paths met, and met the girl in white once again. She told him to come back in three days, and that she would meet him there. She also told him that it would take five trips to the stream to treat all the villagers. Five trips later, Yosoji's mother was healed, and so were many other villagers. The villagers thanked him, but he knew that he owed it all to the girl in white. So he went back to the stream to give thanks to the girl in white. However, he found that the stream was completely dried up and the girl was nowhere to be seen. Thus, Yosoji prayed that the girl would reveal herself, so that he might thank her for her actions. The girl appeared before him and Yosoji said that he wished to tell her how deeply grateful he was to her and that he wished to know who she was, so he could tell the villagers whom to thank. The girl in white said that her identity was not important and said farewell to Yosoji. She then swung a branch of {{ill|Camellia|en|Camellia|WD=Q212815|lt=Camellia|1=Camellia}} over her head and a cloud from Mount Fuji came down and picked her up. Yosoji realized the woman was none other than the Goddess of Mount Fuji.{{cite book|title=Ancient Tales and Folklore of Japan|author=[[Richard Gordon Smith]]|year=1908|chapter-url=https://books.google.com/books?id=HlAqAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA189|chapter=Yosoji's Camellia Tree|others=Illustrated by {{ill|Ella Du Cane|en|Ella Du Cane|WD=Q5364488|lt=Ella Du Cane|1=Ella Du Cane}}|via=Google Books}} ==Genealogy== {{Three generations of Hyuga}} ==In popular culture== In ''[[Persona 4]]'' Konohana Sakuya is a persona owned by {{ill|Yukiko Amagi|en|Yukiko Amagi|WD=Q89513574|lt=Yukiko Amagi|1=Yukiko Amagi}}.{{cite book |title=Persona 4 Visual Data |publisher=Atlus |year=2008 |page=60}} == References == {{reflist}} == External links == {{wiktionary|Konohananosakuya-hime}} * {{cite web|title=FUJISANHONGU SENGENTAISHA| language=English| url=http://www.fuji-hongu.or.jp/sengen/english/index.html| accessdate = 2021-03-01}} - Official homepage of the Asama shrines near Mount Fuji {{jmyth navbox long}} {{Asama Faith}} {{Iwanaga Faith}} [[wiktionary:木花咲耶姫|木花咲耶姫]] [[wiktionary:木花開耶姫|木花開耶姫]] [[en:Konohanasakuya-hime]] [[az:Konohanasakuya]] [[ca:Konohana no Sakuya hime]] {{Commons category|Konohanasakuya-hime}} [[de:Konohanasakuyahime]] [[fa:کونوهاناساکویا-هیمه]] [[fr:Konohana-no-Sakuya]] [[ja:コノハナノサクヤビメ]] [[ko:고노하나노사쿠야비메]] [[mni:ꯀꯣꯅꯣꯍꯅꯁꯀꯨꯌ-ꯍꯤꯃꯦ]] [[pl:Konohanasakuya-hime]] [[ru:Коноханасакуя-химэ]] [[sv:Konohanasakuyahime]] [[th:เซ็นเง็ง]] [[vi:Konohana Sakuya Hime]] [[zh:木花開耶姬]] {{wikidata link|Q1781862}} [[Category:Asama shrines]] [[Category:Fuji worship]] [[Category:Japanese goddesses]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Mount Fuji]] [[Category:Mountain goddesses]] [[Category:Nature goddesses]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Volcano goddesses]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

Sometime between the 14th and 16th centuries, the belief arose among the people of the region that she would protect them from eruptions of the volcano as she had her newborn son from the flames of the burning bower." Konohana is now the principal goddess of Mount Fuji. Members of Fuji-ko have altars in their own home in which they worship Konohana Sakuya Hime. This group also lights a torch for Konohana Sakuya Hime at the fire ceremony at Fuji-Yoshida. but shrines to her at {{ill|Mount Kirishima|en|Mount Kirishima|WD=Q1319361|lt=Kirishima|1=Mount Kirishima}} have been repeatedly destroyed by [[:en:Volcanic|Volcanic]] eruptions. She is also known for having torn up the [[Yatsugatake Mountains]], because it was higher than Fujiyama. == Name and etymology == Konohanasakuya-hime or Konohananosakuya-hime ( or ; lit. "''[cherry] tree blossom blooming princess''" (''princess'', or ''lady of high birth'' explains {{ill|Hime|en|Hime|WD=Q3889712|lt=Hime|1=Hime}}; her name also appears in a shorter form as "''Sakuya-hime''",{{cite web|title=Creation stories|url=http://www.gly.uga.edu/railsback/CS/CSJapan.html|access-date=20 January 2016|website=gly.uga.edu/|archive-date=27 January 2016|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160127071536/http://www.gly.uga.edu/railsback/CS/CSJapan.html|url-status=dead}} and she is also called ''Sengen''{{cite web|author=Cartwright, M ark|date=12 April 2017|title=Mount Fuji|url=https://www.worldhistory.org/Mount_Fuji/|access-date=16 August 2019|publisher=Ancient History}})) == Myths == === Ninigi and Sakuya-hime === Sakuya-hime met [[Ninigi-no-Mikoto|Ninigi]] on the seashore and they fell in love; Ninigi asked [[Ōyamatsumi|Ohoyamatsumi]], the father of Sakuya-hime for her hand in marriage. Oho-Yama proposed his older daughter, Iwa-Naga-hime, instead, but Ninigi had his heart set on Sakuya-hime. Oho-Yama reluctantly agreed and Ninigi and Ko-no-hana married. Because Ninigi refused Iwa-Naga, the rock-princess, human lives are said to be short and fleeting, like the ''sakura'' blossoms, instead of enduring and long lasting, like stones. Sakuya-hime became pregnant in just one night, causing suspicion in Ninigi. He wondered if this was the child of another [[kami]]. Sakuya-hime was enraged at Ninigi's accusation and entered a doorless hut, which she then set fire to, declaring that the child would not be hurt if it were truly the offspring of Ninigi. Inside the hut, Ko-no-hana had three sons, {{ill|Hoderi|en|Hoderi|WD=Q2716491|lt=Hoderi|1=Hoderi}}, [[Hosuseri]] and [[Hoori]].''Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697'', translated from the original Chinese and Japanese by [[William George Aston]]. Book II, page 73. Tuttle Publishing. Tra edition (July 2005). First edition published 1972. {{ISBN|978-0-8048-3674-6}}"According to the 'Kojiki', the great 8th century A.D. compilation of Japanese mythology, Konohana Sakuya-hime married a god who grew suspicious of her when she became pregnant shortly after their wedding. To prove her fidelity to her husband, she entered a benign {{ill|Bedroom|en|Bedroom|WD=Q193837|lt=bower|1=Bedroom}} and miraculously gave birth to a son, unscathed by the surrounding flames. The fire ceremony at Fuji-Yyoshida recalls this story as a means of protecting the town from fire and promoting easy childbirth among women." === Yosoji's camellia tree === {{One source section | date = August 2021 }}[[File:YOSOJI'S CAMELLIA TREE.jpg|left|thumb|Konohanasakuya-hime guiding Yosoji to the stream
{{right|{{small|—illustration by {{ill|Ella Du Cane|en|Ella Du Cane|WD=Q5364488|lt=Ella Du Cane|1=Ella Du Cane}}}}}}]] According to an ddth-century legend, a small village in the {{ill|Suruga Province|en|Suruga Province|WD=Q864170|lt=Suruga Province|1=Suruga Province}} named Kamiide was struck by {{ill|Smallpox|en|Smallpox|WD=Q12214|lt=Smallpox|1=Smallpox}} {{ill|Epidemic|en|Epidemic|WD=Q44512|lt=Epidemic|1=Epidemic}}. In the village, there lived a young boy named Yosoji and his mother, who was infected by the disease. To cure his mother, Yosoji went to a fortune teller who told him to give his mother water from a small {{ill|Stream|en|Stream|WD=Q47521|lt=Stream|1=Stream}} near {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji|1=Mount Fuji}}. [[File:Fuji goddess flies.jpg|thumb|right|Konohanasakuya-hime flying]] The next day, Yosoji decided to go to the river, but then stumbled upon three different paths, not knowing which one to take. A young girl in white came towards Yosoji from the forest and guided him to the stream. Yosoji scooped up water from the stream and gave it to his mother to drink. Days later, Yosoji went back to the area where the three paths met, and met the girl in white once again. She told him to come back in three days, and that she would meet him there. She also told him that it would take five trips to the stream to treat all the villagers. Five trips later, Yosoji's mother was healed, and so were many other villagers. The villagers thanked him, but he knew that he owed it all to the girl in white. So he went back to the stream to give thanks to the girl in white. However, he found that the stream was completely dried up and the girl was nowhere to be seen. Thus, Yosoji prayed that the girl would reveal herself, so that he might thank her for her actions. The girl appeared before him and Yosoji said that he wished to tell her how deeply grateful he was to her and that he wished to know who she was, so he could tell the villagers whom to thank. The girl in white said that her identity was not important and said farewell to Yosoji. She then swung a branch of {{ill|Camellia|en|Camellia|WD=Q212815|lt=Camellia|1=Camellia}} over her head and a cloud from Mount Fuji came down and picked her up. Yosoji realized the woman was none other than the Goddess of Mount Fuji.{{cite book|title=Ancient Tales and Folklore of Japan|author=[[Richard Gordon Smith]]|year=1908|chapter-url=https://books.google.com/books?id=HlAqAAAAYAAJ&pg=PA189|chapter=Yosoji's Camellia Tree|others=Illustrated by {{ill|Ella Du Cane|en|Ella Du Cane|WD=Q5364488|lt=Ella Du Cane|1=Ella Du Cane}}|via=Google Books}} ==Genealogy== {{Three generations of Hyuga}} ==In popular culture== In ''[[Persona 4]]'' Konohana Sakuya is a persona owned by {{ill|Yukiko Amagi|en|Yukiko Amagi|WD=Q89513574|lt=Yukiko Amagi|1=Yukiko Amagi}}.{{cite book |title=Persona 4 Visual Data |publisher=Atlus |year=2008 |page=60}} == References == {{reflist}} == External links == {{wiktionary|Konohananosakuya-hime}} * {{cite web|title=FUJISANHONGU SENGENTAISHA| language=English| url=http://www.fuji-hongu.or.jp/sengen/english/index.html| accessdate = 2021-03-01}} - Official homepage of the Asama shrines near Mount Fuji {{jmyth navbox long}} {{Asama Faith}} {{Iwanaga Faith}} [[wiktionary:木花咲耶姫|木花咲耶姫]] [[wiktionary:木花開耶姫|木花開耶姫]] [[en:Konohanasakuya-hime]] [[az:Konohanasakuya]] [[ca:Konohana no Sakuya hime]] {{Commons category|Konohanasakuya-hime}} [[de:Konohanasakuyahime]] [[fa:کونوهاناساکویا-هیمه]] [[fr:Konohana-no-Sakuya]] [[ja:コノハナノサクヤビメ]] [[ko:고노하나노사쿠야비메]] [[mni:ꯀꯣꯅꯣꯍꯅꯁꯀꯨꯌ-ꯍꯤꯃꯦ]] [[pl:Konohanasakuya-hime]] [[ru:Коноханасакуя-химэ]] [[sv:Konohanasakuyahime]] [[th:เซ็นเง็ง]] [[vi:Konohana Sakuya Hime]] [[zh:木花開耶姬]] {{wikidata link|Q1781862}} [[Category:Asama shrines]] [[Category:Fuji worship]] [[Category:Japanese goddesses]] [[Category:Kunitsukami]] [[Category:Mount Fuji]] [[Category:Mountain goddesses]] [[Category:Nature goddesses]] [[Category:Shinto kami]] [[Category:Volcano goddesses]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

[[az:Konohanasakuya]][[ca:Konohana no Sakuya hime]][[co:commons:Category:Konohanasakuya-hime]][[de:Konohanasakuyahime]][[en:Konohanasakuya-hime]][[fa:کونوهاناساکویا-هیمه]][[fr:Konohana-no-Sakuya]][[ja:コノハナノサクヤビメ]][[ko:고노하나노사쿠야비메]][[mni:ꯀꯣꯅꯣꯍꯅꯁꯀꯨꯌ-ꯍꯤꯃꯦ]][[pl:Konohanasakuya-hime]][[ru:Коноханасакуя-химэ]][[sv:Konohanasakuyahime]][[th:เซ็นเง็ง]][[vi:Konohana Sakuya Hime]][[zh:木花開耶姬]] [[az:Konohanasakuya]][[ca:Konohana no Sakuya hime]][[co:commons:Category:Konohanasakuya-hime]][[de:Konohanasakuyahime]][[en:Konohanasakuya-hime]][[fa:کونوهاناساکویا-هیمه]][[fr:Konohana-no-Sakuya]][[ja:コノハナノサクヤビメ]][[ko:고노하나노사쿠야비메]][[mni:ꯀꯣꯅꯣꯍꯅꯁꯀꯨꯌ-ꯍꯤꯃꯦ]][[pl:Konohanasakuya-hime]][[ru:Коноханасакуя-химэ]][[sv:Konohanasakuyahime]][[th:เซ็นเง็ง]][[vi:Konohana Sakuya Hime]][[zh:木花開耶姬]] [[az:Konohanasakuya]][[ca:Konohana no Sakuya hime]][[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Konohanasakuya-hime]][[de:Konohanasakuyahime]][[en:Konohanasakuya-hime]][[fa:کونوهاناساکویا-هیمه]][[fr:Konohana-no-Sakuya]][[ja:コノハナノサクヤビメ]][[ko:고노하나노사쿠야비메]][[mni:ꯀꯣꯅꯣꯍꯅꯁꯀꯨꯌ-ꯍꯤꯃꯦ]][[pl:Konohanasakuya-hime]][[ru:Коноханасакуя-химэ]][[sv:Konohanasakuyahime]][[th:เซ็นเง็ง]][[vi:Konohana Sakuya Hime]][[zh:木花開耶姬]] {{Short description|Goddess of Mount Fuji in Shinto}} {{More citations needed|date=August 2021}} {{Infobox deity | type = Shinto | name = Konohanasakuya-hime | deity_of = Nature deity | member_of = | image =Konohana Sakuyahime.jpg; File:Konohana Sakuyahime.jpg| alt = | caption = Konohanasakuya-hime, print by {{ill|Hokusai|en|Hokusai|qid=Q5586|lt=Hokusai|1=Hokusai}}, 1834 | other_names = Konohananosakuya-hime
